questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Given below are two statements: Statement I: A hypothetical diatomic molecule with bond order zero is quite stable. Statement II: As bond order increases, the bond length increases. In the light of the above statements:

options.A: Statement I is false but Statement II is true
options.B: Both Statement I and Statement II are true
options.C: Both Statement I and Statement II are false
options.D: Statement I is true but Statement II is false

questions.explanation: Correct answer is C: Both Statement I and Statement II are false (NEET 2025).

options.A: Statement I is incorrect but Statement II is correct
options.B: Both Statement I and Statement II are correct
options.C: Both Statement I and Statement II are incorrect
options.D: Statement I is correct but Statement II is incorrect

questions.explanation: Correct answer is D: Statement I is correct but Statement II is incorrect (NEET 2025).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Given below are two statements: Statement-I: Benzenediazonium salt is prepared by the reaction of aniline with nitrous acid at 273-278 K. It decomposes easily in the dry state. Statement-II: Insertion of iodine into the benzene ring is difficult and hence iodobenzene is prepared through the reaction of benzenediazonium salt with KI.

options.A: Statement I is incorrect but Statement II is correct
options.B: Both Statement I and Statement II are correct
options.C: Both Statement I and Statement II are incorrect
options.D: Statement I is correct but Statement II is incorrect

questions.explanation: Correct answer is B: Both Statement I and Statement II are correct (NEET 2025).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Identify the suitable reagent for the following conversion: Ester to Aldehyde

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Assertion (A): Ethyl iodide undergoes SN2 reaction faster than Ethyl chloride. Reason (R): Iodine is a better leaving group because of its large size.

options.A: A is false but R is true
options.B: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
options.C: Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A
options.D: A is true but R is false

questions.explanation: Correct answer is B: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A (NEET 2025).

questions.explanation: Correct answer is D: HCOOH > CH₃COOH > (CH₃)₂CHCOOH > (CH₃)₃CCOOH (NEET 2025).

questions.explanation: A, D only

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: 5 moles of liquid X and 10 moles of liquid Y make a solution having a vapour pressure of 70 torr. The vapour pressures of pure X and Y are 63 torr and 78 torr respectively. Which of the following is true regarding the described solution?

options.A: The solution has volume greater than the sum of individual volumes

questions.explanation: The solution shows negative deviation

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: According to Kohlrausch's law of independent migration of ions, the molar conductivity at infinite dilution (Λ°m) of a weak electrolyte can be calculated from:

options.A: Direct measurement at very low concentrations
options.B: Sum of the limiting molar conductivities of its constituent ions
options.C: The product of limiting molar conductivities of its constituent ions
options.D: Extrapolation of concentration vs conductivity graph

questions.explanation: Correct answer is B. NCERT Reference: Class 12 Chemistry, Chapter 3 — Electrochemistry, Section 3.4.3: Kohlrausch's Law
questions.ncert_line: According to Kohlrausch's law, at infinite dilution, each ion makes a definite contribution to the molar conductivity of an electrolyte, irrespective of the nature of the other ion.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: If the half-life of a first order reaction is 1 minute, the time required for 99.9% completion is approximately:
questions.explanation: Correct answer is D. NCERT Reference: Class 12 Chemistry, Chapter 4 — Chemical Kinetics, Section 4.2: First Order Reactions
questions.ncert_line: For a first order reaction, the integrated rate equation is: k = (2.303/t) log ([R]₀/[R]). The half-life of a first order reaction is: t₁/₂ = 0.693/k. It is independent of initial concentration.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Which of the following correctly describes D-fructose?

options.B: A ketohexose that is laevorotatory, found in honey
options.C: An aldohexose that reduces Fehling's solution
options.D: A disaccharide formed by glucose and galactose

questions.explanation: Correct answer is B. NCERT Reference: Class 12 Chemistry, Chapter 14 — Biomolecules, Section 14.1.2: Monosaccharides
questions.ncert_line: Fructose is a ketohexose. It is found in honey and fruits. It is laevorotatory and exists in α and β anomeric forms due to the presence of an anomeric carbon.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Which of the following is the correct decreasing order of basic strength?
questions.explanation: Correct answer is D. NCERT Reference: Class 12 Chemistry, Chapter 13 — Amines, Section 13.4: Chemical Reactions of Amines
questions.ncert_line: The basic character of amines depends on the availability of the lone pair of electrons on nitrogen. Aliphatic amines are more basic than aromatic amines due to the delocalization of the lone pair in aromatic amines.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: The degree of dissociation (α) of a weak electrolyte can be calculated using the formula α = Λm/Λ°m. What does Λ°m represent?

options.A: Molar conductivity at a given concentration
options.B: Limiting molar conductivity at infinite dilution

questions.explanation: Correct: B. Ref: Class 12 Chemistry, Chapter 3 — Electrochemistry, Section 3.4: Conductance of Electrolytic Solutions
questions.ncert_line: The degree of dissociation (α) of a weak electrolyte is equal to the ratio of its molar conductivity at a given concentration (Λm) to its limiting molar conductivity (Λ°m): α = Λm / Λ°m.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Why can the molar conductivity at infinite dilution (Λ°m) for weak electrolytes NOT be determined by direct measurement?

options.A: Weak electrolytes decompose at infinite dilution
options.C: The curve does not show a clear limiting value when extrapolated
options.D: Weak electrolytes form precipitates at very low concentrations

questions.explanation: Correct: C. Ref: Class 12 Chemistry, Chapter 3 — Electrochemistry, Section 3.4.3: Kohlrausch's Law
questions.ncert_line: Kohlrausch's law allows calculation of Λ°m for weak electrolytes which cannot be obtained by direct measurement. For CH3COOH: Λ°m(CH3COOH) = Λ°m(CH3COONa) + Λ°m(HCl) − Λ°m(NaCl).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: According to Faraday's second law of electrolysis, the amounts of different substances deposited by the same quantity of electricity are proportional to their:
questions.explanation: Correct: B. Ref: Class 12 Chemistry, Chapter 3 — Electrochemistry, Section 3.2: Electrolytic Cells and Electrolysis
questions.ncert_line: Faraday's first law: The amount of chemical reaction at an electrode is proportional to the quantity of electricity passed. Faraday's second law: The amounts of different substances deposited by the same quantity of electricity are proportional to their equivalent masses.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Which of the following processes follows first order kinetics?

options.A: Decomposition of HI on a gold surface (zero order)
options.C: Combination of H₂ and I₂ to form HI
options.D: Decomposition of N2O5 (which is second order)

questions.explanation: Correct: B. Ref: Class 12 Chemistry, Chapter 4 — Chemical Kinetics, Section 4.2: First Order Reactions
questions.ncert_line: For a first order reaction: k = (2.303/t) log([R]₀/[R]). The half-life t₁/₂ = 0.693/k is independent of initial concentration. All radioactive decays follow first order kinetics.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: According to collision theory, the fraction of molecules that actually react upon collision is low because:

options.A: Most molecules move too slowly to collide
options.B: Most collisions lack sufficient energy AND proper orientation
options.C: The activation energy barrier does not exist for most reactions
options.D: Collision frequency is very low at room temperature

questions.explanation: Correct: B. Ref: Class 12 Chemistry, Chapter 4 — Chemical Kinetics, Section 4.5: Collision Theory
questions.ncert_line: According to collision theory, reactant molecules must collide with sufficient energy (≥ activation energy) and with proper orientation for a reaction to occur. Not every collision leads to a reaction.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a colligative property?
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Ref: Class 12 Chemistry, Chapter 2 — Solutions, Section 2.5: Colligative Properties
questions.ncert_line: Colligative properties depend only on the number of solute particles, not their nature. They include: relative lowering of vapour pressure, elevation of boiling point, depression of freezing point, and osmotic pressure.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Which aqueous solution would exhibit the highest boiling point?
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Ref: Class 12 Chemistry, Chapter 2 — Solutions, Section 2.5: Abnormal Molar Masses
questions.ncert_line: The Van't Hoff factor (i) accounts for dissociation or association of solutes. For electrolytes that dissociate, i > 1. For solutes that associate (e.g., acetic acid in benzene), i < 1. For non-electrolytes, i = 1.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Henry's law states that the solubility of a gas in a liquid at constant temperature is:

options.A: Inversely proportional to the partial pressure of the gas
options.B: Directly proportional to the partial pressure of the gas
options.C: Independent of the partial pressure of the gas
options.D: Proportional to the square of partial pressure

questions.explanation: Correct: B. Ref: Class 12 Chemistry, Chapter 2 — Solutions, Section 2.3: Solubility of Gases in Liquids
questions.ncert_line: Henry's law states that the solubility of a gas in a liquid at constant temperature is directly proportional to the partial pressure of the gas above the solution: p = KH × x (where KH is Henry's constant).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Two solutions of equal osmotic pressure at the same temperature are called:
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Ref: Class 12 Chemistry, Chapter 2 — Solutions, Section 2.5.4: Osmosis and Osmotic Pressure
questions.ncert_line: Osmotic pressure (π) = iCRT, where C is molarity, R is gas constant, T is temperature in Kelvin, and i is Van't Hoff factor. Osmotic pressure is a colligative property.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: For the reaction PCl₅(g) ⇌ PCl₃(g) + Cl₂(g), the relationship between Kp and Kc at temperature T is:
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Ref: Class 11 Chemistry, Chapter 7 — Equilibrium, Section 7.5: Equilibrium Constant
questions.ncert_line: The relationship between Kp and Kc is: Kp = Kc(RT)^Δn, where Δn is the difference in moles of gaseous products and reactants. When Δn = 0, Kp = Kc.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: At 298 K, the value of the ionic product of water (Kw) is:
questions.explanation: Correct: B. Ref: Class 11 Chemistry, Chapter 7 — Equilibrium, Section 7.11.1: Ionisation of Water
questions.ncert_line: The ionic product of water (Kw) = [H+][OH−] = 1.0 × 10⁻¹⁴ at 298 K. In a neutral solution, [H+] = [OH−] = 1.0 × 10⁻⁷ M. At temperatures above 298 K, Kw increases.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: An acidic buffer solution is prepared by mixing:

options.A: A strong acid and its salt with a strong base
options.B: A weak acid and its salt with a strong base
options.C: A weak base and its salt with a strong acid
options.D: Equal volumes of a strong acid and strong base

questions.explanation: Correct: B. Ref: Class 11 Chemistry, Chapter 7 — Equilibrium, Section 7.11.4: Buffer Solutions
questions.ncert_line: A buffer solution resists changes in pH on addition of small amounts of acid or base. An acidic buffer is a mixture of a weak acid and its salt with a strong base (e.g., CH3COOH/CH3COONa). Its pH = pKa + log([salt]/[acid]).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: A process is spontaneous at constant temperature and pressure when:
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Ref: Class 11 Chemistry, Chapter 6 — Thermodynamics, Section 6.8: Gibbs Energy and Spontaneity
questions.ncert_line: Gibbs energy: G = H − TS. For a spontaneous process at constant T and P, ΔG < 0. At equilibrium, ΔG = 0. The relationship between ΔG° and equilibrium constant K is: ΔG° = −RT ln K.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Which of the following processes results in an INCREASE in entropy?

options.A: Condensation of water vapour to liquid
options.B: Crystallisation of a salt from solution
options.D: Dissolution of NaCl in water does NOT increase entropy

questions.explanation: Correct: C. Ref: Class 11 Chemistry, Chapter 6 — Thermodynamics, Section 6.7: Entropy
questions.ncert_line: Entropy (S) is a measure of the degree of disorder or randomness of a system. The entropy of the universe increases for all spontaneous processes (ΔS_universe > 0). Entropy of a pure crystalline substance at 0 K is zero (Third Law).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Hess's law is a consequence of which law of thermodynamics?
questions.explanation: Correct: B. Ref: Class 11 Chemistry, Chapter 6 — Thermodynamics, Section 6.1: Hess's Law
questions.ncert_line: Hess's law states that the enthalpy change for a reaction is the same whether it occurs in one step or in several steps. ΔH°reaction = ΣΔH°f(products) − ΣΔH°f(reactants).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Which set of quantum numbers is NOT possible for an electron?
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Ref: Class 11 Chemistry, Chapter 2 — Structure of Atom, Section 2.6: Quantum Mechanical Model
questions.ncert_line: Four quantum numbers describe an electron: principal (n), azimuthal (l, 0 to n−1), magnetic (ml, −l to +l), and spin (ms, +½ or −½). No two electrons in an atom can have the same set of four quantum numbers (Pauli's exclusion principle).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: The de Broglie wavelength of a particle with momentum p is:
questions.explanation: Correct: B. Ref: Class 11 Chemistry, Chapter 2 — Structure of Atom, Section 2.5.1: de Broglie Relationship
questions.ncert_line: de Broglie proposed that matter has wave-particle duality. The de Broglie wavelength: λ = h/mv = h/p, where h is Planck's constant and mv is momentum. This applies to all matter but is significant only for very small particles.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Heisenberg's uncertainty principle states that it is impossible to simultaneously determine precisely:

options.A: The mass and velocity of a particle
options.B: The position and momentum of a subatomic particle
options.C: The energy and temperature of a system
options.D: The charge and mass of an electron

questions.explanation: Correct: B. Ref: Class 11 Chemistry, Chapter 2 — Structure of Atom, Section 2.5.2: Heisenberg's Uncertainty Principle
questions.ncert_line: Heisenberg's uncertainty principle: It is impossible to simultaneously determine precisely both the position and momentum of a subatomic particle: Δx × Δp ≥ h/4π.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question:
Statement I: A hypothetical diatomic molecule with bond order zero does not exist (is unstable).
Statement II: As bond order increases, bond length decreases.

options.A: Both Statement I and Statement II are true
options.B: Statement I is true but Statement II is false
options.C: Statement I is false but Statement II is true
options.D: Both Statement I and Statement II are false

questions.explanation: Correct: A. Ref: Class 11 Chemistry, Chapter 4 — Chemical Bonding and Molecular Structure, Section 4.5: Molecular Orbital Theory
questions.ncert_line: Bond order is the number of bonds between a pair of atoms. As bond order increases, bond length decreases and bond enthalpy increases. A molecule with bond order zero does not exist (is unstable).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: According to VSEPR theory, which repulsion is the strongest?
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Ref: Class 11 Chemistry, Chapter 4 — Chemical Bonding and Molecular Structure, Section 4.3: VSEPR Theory
questions.ncert_line: VSEPR theory: The shape of a molecule is determined by the repulsion between electron pairs (bonding and lone pairs) around the central atom. Lone pair-lone pair repulsion > lone pair-bond pair > bond pair-bond pair.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: The hybridisation of carbon in ethyne (HC≡CH) is:
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Ref: Class 11 Chemistry, Chapter 4 — Chemical Bonding and Molecular Structure, Section 4.7: Hybridisation
questions.ncert_line: In sp3 hybridisation, one s and three p orbitals mix to form four equivalent sp3 hybrid orbitals directed toward the corners of a tetrahedron. In sp2, three equivalent orbitals form a trigonal planar arrangement.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Which of the following CANNOT form intermolecular hydrogen bonds?
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Ref: Class 11 Chemistry, Chapter 4 — Chemical Bonding and Molecular Structure, Section 4.9: Hydrogen Bonding
questions.ncert_line: Hydrogen bond forms between highly electronegative atoms (F, O, N) and hydrogen bonded to another electronegative atom. Intermolecular H-bonding raises boiling point. Intramolecular H-bonding (as in o-nitrophenol) lowers boiling point.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: The first ionisation enthalpy of oxygen (O) is lower than that of nitrogen (N). The correct reason is:

options.A: Oxygen has more electrons than nitrogen
options.B: Nitrogen has a stable half-filled 2p subshell (2p³) making it harder to remove an electron
options.C: Oxygen has a larger atomic radius than nitrogen
options.D: The 2p orbital of nitrogen is higher in energy than that of oxygen

questions.explanation: Correct: B. Ref: Class 11 Chemistry, Chapter 3 — Classification of Elements and Periodicity, Section 3.7: Periodic Trends
questions.ncert_line: Ionisation enthalpy generally increases across a period (left to right) and decreases down a group. There are exceptions: IE of Be > B (B has a 2p electron which is easier to remove than 2s) and IE of N > O (N has a stable half-filled 2p).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: On the Pauling scale, which of the following is the correct comparison of electronegativities of N and Cl?

options.A: N and Cl have the same electronegativity (both = 3.0)
options.B: N (3.0) is less electronegative than Cl (3.2)
options.C: N (3.5) is more electronegative than Cl (3.2)
options.D: Cl is more electronegative than F

questions.explanation: Correct: B. Ref: Class 11 Chemistry, Chapter 3 — Classification of Elements and Periodicity, Section 3.7.3: Electronegativity
questions.ncert_line: Electronegativity increases across a period and decreases down a group. Fluorine (F) is the most electronegative element. The electronegativity of N and Cl are different on the Pauling scale (N = 3.0, Cl = 3.2).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Anti-Markovnikov addition of HBr to propene occurs in the presence of peroxides. This mechanism involves:
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Ref: Class 11 Chemistry, Chapter 13 — Hydrocarbons, Section 13.2: Free Radical Addition
questions.ncert_line: In the presence of peroxides (ROOR), HBr adds to alkenes in an anti-Markovnikov fashion (free radical addition). The bromine attaches to the less substituted carbon. This only applies to HBr, NOT HCl or HI.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Which of the following can show cis-trans (geometric) isomerism?
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Ref: Class 11 Chemistry, Chapter 13 — Hydrocarbons, Section 13.4: Geometrical Isomerism
questions.ncert_line: Cis-trans (geometric) isomerism occurs in alkenes when each carbon of the double bond has two different substituents, OR in cyclic compounds when substituents are on different carbons of the ring. 1,2-dimethylcyclohexane shows cis-trans isomerism.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: SN2 reactions proceed with which stereochemical outcome?
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Ref: Class 12 Chemistry, Chapter 10 — Haloalkanes and Haloarenes, Section 10.3.1: Nucleophilic Substitution
questions.ncert_line: SN1 reactions proceed via a carbocation intermediate (two steps). They are favoured by tertiary substrates and polar protic solvents. SN2 reactions are concerted (one step) and proceed with inversion of configuration (Walden inversion). They are favoured by primary substrates.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question:
Assertion (A): Ethyl iodide undergoes SN2 reaction faster than ethyl chloride.
Reason (R): Iodide is a better leaving group than chloride due to its larger size and weaker C−I bond.

options.A: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
options.B: Both A and R are true but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
options.C: A is true but R is false
options.D: A is false but R is true

questions.explanation: Correct: A. Ref: Class 12 Chemistry, Chapter 10 — Haloalkanes and Haloarenes, Section 10.3: Reactions of Haloalkanes
questions.ncert_line: A good leaving group is one that can carry away the electron pair and form a stable species. The order of leaving group ability: I⁻ > Br⁻ > Cl⁻ > F⁻. Iodide is the best leaving group because of its large size and weak C−I bond.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: According to Saytzeff's rule, the predominant product of dehydrohalogenation is:

options.D: A mixture of equal amounts of all possible alkenes

questions.explanation: Correct: B. Ref: Class 12 Chemistry, Chapter 10 — Haloalkanes and Haloarenes, Section 10.3.2: Elimination Reactions
questions.ncert_line: Saytzeff's rule states that in elimination reactions, the predominant alkene formed is the more substituted (more stable) one. Dehydrohalogenation of 2-bromobutane gives but-2-ene (major) rather than but-1-ene.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Phenol is more acidic than ethanol because:

options.A: Phenol has a higher molecular weight
options.B: The phenoxide ion is stabilised by resonance delocalization into the benzene ring
options.C: Phenol has an −OH group attached to an aromatic ring
options.D: The O−H bond in phenol is weaker than in ethanol

questions.explanation: Correct: B. Ref: Class 12 Chemistry, Chapter 11 — Alcohols, Phenols and Ethers, Section 11.3.2: Reactions of Phenols
questions.ncert_line: Phenol is more acidic than alcohol (pKa ~10) because the phenoxide ion is stabilised by resonance delocalization of the negative charge into the benzene ring. Phenol undergoes electrophilic substitution preferentially at ortho and para positions.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: The correct decreasing order of acidity among the following aliphatic acids is:

options.D: All four acids have equal acidity

questions.explanation: Correct: B. Ref: Class 12 Chemistry, Chapter 12 — Aldehydes, Ketones and Carboxylic Acids, Section 12.5.2: Reactions of Carboxylic Acids
questions.ncert_line: The acidity of carboxylic acids decreases with increasing alkyl chain length (electron-donating inductive effect). The order: HCOOH > CH3COOH > CH3CH2COOH. Electron-withdrawing groups on the α-carbon increase acidity.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Aldol condensation requires a carbonyl compound with at least one:
questions.explanation: Correct: B. Ref: Class 12 Chemistry, Chapter 12 — Aldehydes, Ketones and Carboxylic Acids, Section 12.3: Aldol Condensation
questions.ncert_line: In the aldol condensation, an aldehyde or ketone with an α-hydrogen reacts with another carbonyl compound in the presence of base to give a β-hydroxy carbonyl compound (aldol). On heating, dehydration occurs to give an α,β-unsaturated compound.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: What type of alcohol is formed when excess CH₃MgBr reacts with a nitrile (R-CN) followed by H₃O⁺ hydrolysis?
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Ref: Class 12 Chemistry, Chapter 11 — Alcohols, Phenols and Ethers, Section 11.2.3: Reactions of Organometallic Compounds
questions.ncert_line: Grignard reagents (RMgX) act as nucleophiles and react with carbonyl compounds. Addition to an aldehyde gives a secondary alcohol; addition to a ketone gives a tertiary alcohol; addition to a nitrile (RCN) gives a ketone after hydrolysis.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Which of the following reactions does NOT give a primary amine as the product?

options.D: Reductive amination of a secondary amine

questions.explanation: Correct: D. Ref: Class 12 Chemistry, Chapter 13 — Amines, Section 13.2: Preparation of Amines
questions.ncert_line: Primary amines can be prepared by: (1) reduction of nitro compounds, (2) reduction of nitriles with LiAlH4, (3) reduction of amides (Hofmann bromamide reaction gives primary amine with one less C), (4) Gabriel synthesis.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Benzenediazonium salt is prepared at 0−5°C. What happens if the temperature is raised above 5°C during diazotisation?

options.A: The reaction becomes faster and more efficient
options.B: The diazonium salt decomposes (hydrolyses to phenol)
options.C: The amine gets oxidised to nitrobenzene
options.D: The salt precipitates out of solution

questions.explanation: Correct: B. Ref: Class 12 Chemistry, Chapter 13 — Amines, Section 13.5: Diazonium Salts
questions.ncert_line: Benzenediazonium salt is prepared by the reaction of aniline with NaNO2 and HCl at 0−5°C (diazotisation). It is unstable and decomposes easily in the dry state. It is a versatile intermediate for the synthesis of aryl halides, phenol, cyanobenzene and azo dyes.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: In an octahedral complex, the d orbitals split into two sets. Which set has HIGHER energy?

options.D: The splitting depends only on the metal ion

questions.explanation: Correct: B. Ref: Class 12 Chemistry, Chapter 9 — Coordination Compounds, Section 9.7.2: Crystal Field Theory
questions.ncert_line: In an octahedral complex, d orbitals split into two sets: the lower energy t2g (dxy, dxz, dyz) and the higher energy eg (dx²−y², dz²). The energy difference is Δo (crystal field splitting energy). Weak field ligands give small Δo (high spin); strong field ligands give large Δo (low spin).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Which ligand causes the LARGEST crystal field splitting (Δ)?
questions.explanation: Correct: D. Ref: Class 12 Chemistry, Chapter 9 — Coordination Compounds, Section 9.7.2: Spectrochemical Series
questions.ncert_line: The spectrochemical series orders ligands by crystal field splitting: I⁻ < Br⁻ < Cl⁻ < F⁻ < OH⁻ < H₂O < NH₃ < en < NO₂⁻ < CN⁻ < CO. Strong field ligands (CN⁻, CO) cause large splitting and low spin complexes.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: In IUPAC nomenclature of coordination compounds, the name of the neutral ligand ammonia (NH₃) within a complex is:
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Ref: Class 12 Chemistry, Chapter 9 — Coordination Compounds, Section 9.3: Nomenclature of Coordination Compounds
questions.ncert_line: In naming coordination compounds, the cation is named before the anion. Within the complex ion, ligands are named before the metal. Anionic ligands end in -o; neutral ligands use their regular names (except water = aqua, ammonia = ammine, CO = carbonyl).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: The shape of XeOF₄ is:
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Ref: Class 12 Chemistry, Chapter 7 — The p-Block Elements, Section 7.5: Noble Gases
questions.ncert_line: XeOF4 has 5 bonding pairs and 1 lone pair around Xe — the lone pair occupies one axial position of an octahedron, giving XeOF4 a square pyramidal shape. XeF2 is linear (3 lone pairs + 2 bond pairs = linear).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: ClF₃ is an interhalogen compound. Its shape is:
questions.explanation: Correct: B. Ref: Class 12 Chemistry, Chapter 7 — The p-Block Elements, Section 7.4.6: Interhalogen Compounds
questions.ncert_line: Interhalogen compounds are formed between two different halogens. The general formula is XX'n where n = 1, 3, 5 or 7. ClF3 has a T-shape, IF5 has a square pyramidal shape, and IF7 has pentagonal bipyramidal geometry.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: In which of the following oxoacids does sulphur have the oxidation state +6?
questions.explanation: Correct: B. Ref: Class 12 Chemistry, Chapter 7 — The p-Block Elements, Section 7.3: Oxoacids of Sulphur
questions.ncert_line: The important oxoacids of sulphur are H2SO3 (sulphurous acid, S in +4 state), H2SO4 (sulphuric acid, S in +6 state), H2S2O7 (pyrosulphuric acid/oleum), and H2S2O8 (peroxodisulphuric acid).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Which of the following is a NON-reducing sugar?
questions.explanation: Correct: D. Ref: Class 12 Chemistry, Chapter 14 — Biomolecules, Section 14.1.4: Disaccharides
questions.ncert_line: Reducing sugars are carbohydrates that can reduce Fehling's solution or Tollens' reagent. All monosaccharides and most disaccharides (maltose, lactose) are reducing sugars. Sucrose is a non-reducing sugar because its anomeric carbons are involved in the glycosidic bond.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Which of the following vitamins is water-soluble?
questions.explanation: Correct: D. Ref: Class 12 Chemistry, Chapter 14 — Biomolecules, Section 14.4: Vitamins
questions.ncert_line: Fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, K) are stored in the body. Water-soluble vitamins (B complex and C) are not stored and must be taken daily. Vitamin B12 deficiency causes pernicious anaemia. Vitamin D deficiency causes rickets.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: The sugar present in DNA is:
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Ref: Class 12 Chemistry, Chapter 14 — Biomolecules, Section 14.5: Nucleic Acids
questions.ncert_line: A nucleotide consists of a nitrogenous base, a pentose sugar, and a phosphate group. In DNA, the sugar is deoxyribose; in RNA, it is ribose. DNA is double-stranded; most RNA is single-stranded.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: The peptide bond in a protein is formed between which two functional groups?

options.C: −COOH of one amino acid and −NH₂ of another

questions.explanation: Correct: C. Ref: Class 12 Chemistry, Chapter 14 — Biomolecules, Section 14.2: Proteins
questions.ncert_line: Amino acids have both an amino (−NH2) and a carboxyl (−COOH) group. At physiological pH, they exist as zwitterions. The peptide bond is formed by condensation between the −COOH of one amino acid and the −NH2 of another with loss of water.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Nylon-6,6 is synthesised from:
questions.explanation: Correct: B. Ref: Class 12 Chemistry, Chapter 15 — Polymers, Section 15.2: Condensation Polymers
questions.ncert_line: Condensation polymerisation involves the reaction between bifunctional monomers with loss of small molecules (water, HCl, etc.). Nylon-6,6 is formed from hexamethylenediamine and adipic acid. Dacron (terylene) is formed from ethylene glycol and terephthalic acid.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Teflon (PTFE) is an addition polymer of:
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Ref: Class 12 Chemistry, Chapter 15 — Polymers, Section 15.2: Classification of Polymers
questions.ncert_line: Addition polymerisation involves the successive addition of monomer units without the loss of any small molecules. Polythene (addition polymer of ethene), PVC (addition polymer of vinyl chloride), and PTFE (teflon, from tetrafluoroethylene) are examples.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Which of the following reactions does NOT belong to Lassaigne's (sodium fusion) test?

options.A: Na + C + N → NaCN (detection of nitrogen)
options.C: 2CuO + C → 2Cu + CO₂ (this tests for carbon — NOT part of Lassaigne's test)

questions.explanation: Correct: C. Ref: Class 11 Chemistry, Chapter 12 — Organic Chemistry: Some Basic Principles, Section 12.4: Qualitative Analysis
questions.ncert_line: Lassaigne's test (sodium fusion test) is used to detect the presence of halogens, nitrogen, and sulphur in organic compounds. The fusion converts covalently bonded elements into ionic forms: Na + X → NaX; Na + C + N → NaCN; 2Na + S → Na2S.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: A compound with two chiral centres has a maximum of how many stereoisomers?
questions.explanation: Correct: B. Ref: Class 12 Chemistry, Chapter 9 — Coordination Compounds, Section 9.6: Stereoisomerism
questions.ncert_line: A chiral carbon (asymmetric carbon) is one bonded to four different groups. It gives rise to optical isomers (enantiomers). A molecule with n chiral centres has a maximum of 2ⁿ stereoisomers. A meso compound has chiral centres but is achiral due to internal plane of symmetry.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Stereoisomers differ from each other in:
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Ref: Class 11 Chemistry, Chapter 12 — Organic Chemistry: Some Basic Principles, Section 12.6: Stereoisomerism
questions.ncert_line: Stereoisomers have the same molecular formula and same structural formula but differ in the spatial arrangement of atoms. They include configurational isomers (cis-trans and optical isomers) and conformational isomers.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: During electrolysis of dilute H₂SO₄, the gas liberated at the anode is:
